avocado.notes: Mature avocados rarely survive transplanting. Root system is extremely sensitive to disturbance. For trees over 2 years, prioritize fruit harvest over plant rescue. If transplanting young trees, use hydraulic tree spade to keep root ball intact.

citrus.notes: Citrus handles balled-and-burlapped transplanting better than avocado but still risky at maturity. Reduce irrigation 2 weeks before transplant to harden roots. Keep root ball moist during transport.

strawberries.notes: Strawberry crowns and runners transplant very well at any stage. Can be hand-pulled quickly. Wrap roots in moist burlap during transport. Replant within 24 hours for best survival.

tomatoes.notes: Young transplants move well with root ball. Mature fruiting plants can be moved but fruit production stalls 1-2 weeks post-transplant. If fruit is near ripe, harvest first then uproot the plant.

wheat.notes: Wheat cannot be transplanted. Only option is early harvest if mature enough, or disc-plow the field under to remove fuel load and create a firebreak.

corn.notes: Corn cannot be transplanted at any stage. Harvest if within 2 weeks of maturity, otherwise disc-plow the field under to eliminate standing dry biomass that carries fire.

alfalfa.notes: Alfalfa cannot be transplanted. Cut and bale standing crop immediately to remove fuel load. Deep taproot will regrow after fire if crown survives — focus on removing above-ground dry biomass.

grapes.notes: Mature grapevines cannot be transplanted. Harvest fruit if mature. Young vines under 2 years can be pulled bare-root and kept moist. Mature trellis and vine structure creates significant fire fuel — consider cutting vines at base to reduce fuel load.

almonds.notes: Mature almonds almost never survive transplanting. Focus on hull-split harvest if within 3 weeks of maturity. Remove dry hull debris from ground as it is highly flammable.

cover_crop.notes: Cover crops have no salvage value. Mow or disc-plow immediately to eliminate dry standing biomass — this is the single most effective firebreak action for these fields. Dry standing cover crop is the highest fire spread risk on any farm.

potatoes.notes: Potatoes cannot be transplanted but tubers can be mechanically harvested early if close to maturity. Harvest removes the above-ground dry vine biomass which is a fire risk. Underground tubers are protected from fire.
